AI.tldr: The diverse γ-ray pulse morphologies of three young pulsars (Crab, Vela, Dragonfly) are unified by a single four-beam geometric model, where two altitude-separated beam pairs—lower (corotating, producing main peaks) and higher (radially outflowing, producing bridge/shoulder/ripple emission)—explain the phaseograms, with site heights scaling from ~0.7 (Crab, ~1 kyr) to ~1.1–1.4 light-cylinder radii (Vela/Dragonfly, ~10 kyr), providing an observationally anchored framework for young pulsars.
AI.motivation: To understand whether the diverse and energy-dependent γ-ray pulse morphologies of young pulsars (Crab, Vela, Dragonfly) can be organized under a single geometric template, despite their apparent differences.
AI.method: A mechanism-agnostic, geometry-first parametric model applied to Fermi LAT data (60 MeV–3 GeV), incorporating phase-dependent Doppler shifts to constrain 3D locations and bulk motions of four emission sites, fitting the phaseograms of all three pulsars.
AI.result: All three pulsars decompose into two altitude-separated beam pairs: a lower pair (azimuthal corotation, sharp main peaks) and a higher pair (radially outward bulk motion, bridge/shoulder/ripple emission). Higher-altitude site heights range from ~0.7 (Crab) to ~1.1–1.4 light-cylinder radii (Vela/Dragonfly), scaling with pulsar age.
AI.conclusion: A unified four-beam, observation-driven geometry maps an altitude-dependent azimuthal tilt of pulsed γ-ray emission, offering a systematic framework testable and extensible to other young pulsars.

title: A New Perspective on Galactic Evolution: Studying the Outskirts of the Abell S1063 Galaxy Cluster
comment: 12 pages, 15 figures
summary: Galaxy physical properties are influenced by their environments, but the processes responsible for mass and environmental quenching and structural transformations remain debated. Galaxy clusters are ideal laboratories for investigating galaxy formation and evolution, offering a full range of galaxy properties and environments. Observations of large-scale structures, particularly filaments in cluster outskirts ($r \sim5r_{200}$), are currently constrained to the low-redshift Universe. To explore galaxy evolution at intermediate redshifts, deep photometric data, ideally combined with spectroscopic redshifts, are essential. Abell S1063 cluster ($z$ = 0.346) is observed within the Galaxy Assembly as a function of the Mass and Environment program with the VLT Survey Telescope (VST-GAME) combined VISTA Public Survey program Galaxy Cluster At Vircam. We investigate galaxy evolution across a wide range of stellar masses and environments. We release a multiwavelength photometric catalog with photometric redshifts for 64394 sources in $1x1 deg^2$. The analysis of overdensity regions provides insights for future studies on galaxy properties in cluster outskirts. The dataset is obtained through deep ($r<$24.65 mag) and wide optical ($u$, $g$, $r$, $i$, VST) and near-infrared ($Y$, $J$, $K_s$, VISTA) observations. The photometric catalog includes all detected sources, excluding nearby or overlapping objects, saturated stars, and image artifacts. The multiwavelength catalog enabled photometric redshift estimates and identification of cluster members. The density field allowed comparison of galaxy properties, colors, and masses across environments. We detect a very dense structure near the cluster center, and with such a large field of view, we find another dense region to the north-west, in the opposite direction to the cluster elongation. Filaments connecting the regions are also visible.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a multiwavelength photometric catalog (optical+NIR) of 64,394 sources in a 1×1 deg² field around the Abell S1063 cluster (z=0.346) from VST-GAME and VISTA surveys, enabling photometric redshift estimates and identification of cluster members. The analysis reveals a dense central structure, a second dense region to the north-west, and connecting filaments, providing insights into environmental quenching and galaxy evolution across a wide range of stellar masses and environments in cluster outskirts.
AI.motivation: Galaxy physical properties are influenced by their environments, but the processes responsible for mass and environmental quenching and structural transformations remain debated. Galaxy clusters are ideal laboratories for investigating these processes, yet observations of large-scale structures in cluster outskirts at intermediate redshifts are currently constrained.
AI.method: Deep and wide multiwavelength observations using VST (u,g,r,i) and VISTA (Y,J,Ks) covering 1×1 deg²; construction of a photometric catalog with photometric redshifts for 64,394 sources; density field analysis to identify overdense regions and characterize galaxy properties across environments.
AI.result: A multiwavelength photometric catalog with photometric redshifts was released; a very dense structure near the cluster center and another dense region to the north-west were detected; filaments connecting the regions are visible; cluster member identification and comparison of galaxy colors/masses across environments were enabled.
AI.conclusion: The dataset enables studies of galaxy evolution across a wide range of stellar masses and environments at intermediate redshift (z=0.346), providing insights for future work on environmental quenching and structural transformations in cluster outskirts.

title: On the polarization position angle jumps in FRB 20240114A
comment: 16 pages, 10 figures, submitted to Astronomy and Astrophysics
summary:
Fast radio bursts (FRBs), thought to originate from magnetars, exhibit diverse polarization properties that constrain their emission physics and local magneto-ionic environments. The polarization position angle (PPA) is particularly sensitive to magnetic-field geometry in the emitting region and propagation effects in the magnetosphere and beyond. In hyper-active repeaters, PPAs are typically stable within bursts and over timescales of hours to days.
  Here, we present observations of the repeating source FRB~20240114A, which show significant burst-to-burst PPA variations. Using full-Stokes, high-time-resolution observations from the Nançay Radio Telescope (1.1--1.8\,GHz) and the Effelsberg 100-m telescope (1.3--1.5\,GHz) over $\sim1$~year, we measure rotation measures (RMs), polarization fractions, and time-resolved PPAs across 12 epochs.
  The RMs remain stable, and the emission is predominantly highly linearly polarized, with $\sim81\%$ of bursts showing $L/I > 0.8$, while circular polarization is weaker ($\sim16\%$ with $|V/I| > 0.1$). We find no evidence for Faraday conversion.
  The PPA exhibits rapid, stochastic variations from milliseconds to hours, spanning $\pm90^\circ$ during two active periods and $\pm50^\circ$ in a third. The distribution of PPA jumps shows that (1) there is no difference in the distribution of jumps on timescales shorter or longer than 1\,s; (2) positive and negative jumps are equally likely; and (3) a jump of $\pm90^\circ$, as expected from, e.g., orthogonal mode jumps, is not more common than any other value.
  This combination of stable RM, high linear polarization, and extreme PPA variability is not seen in other hyper-active repeaters. These results disfavor emission from a single fixed region and instead suggest multiple emission regions and/or strong magnetospheric and foreground propagation effects, such as plasma lensing.

AI.tldr: FRB 20240114A shows extreme, random PPA variations (±90°) across timescales from milliseconds to hours, while maintaining stable RM and high linear polarization. This unique behavior, unseen in other hyper-active repeaters, disfavors a single fixed emission region and suggests multiple emission sites or strong propagation effects like plasma lensing.
AI.motivation: To understand FRB emission physics and magneto-ionic environments by studying the polarization properties of repeating FRBs, specifically the PPA behavior which is sensitive to magnetic-field geometry and propagation effects.
AI.method: Full-Stokes, high-time-resolution observations of FRB 20240114A using Nançay Radio Telescope (1.1–1.8 GHz) and Effelsberg 100-m telescope (1.3–1.5 GHz) across 12 epochs over ~1 year, measuring RMs, polarization fractions, and time-resolved PPAs.
AI.result: Stable RMs, ~81% bursts with L/I > 0.8, weak circular polarization (~16% with |V/I| > 0.1), no Faraday conversion. PPA shows rapid stochastic variations (±90° in two active periods, ±50° in a third). PPA jumps show no timescale dependence, equal positive/negative probability, and no preference for ±90° orthogonal jumps.
AI.conclusion: The unique combination of stable RM, high linear polarization, and extreme PPA variability disfavors emission from a single fixed region, instead suggesting multiple emission regions and/or strong magnetospheric and foreground propagation effects such as plasma lensing.

title: Applications of 1.4 GHz diagnostics to Type Ia Supernova host galaxies
comment: Accepted for publication in MNRAS, 14 pages, 4 figures (+ 1 figure in the Appendix)
summary: Type Ia supernova (SN Ia) standardisation parameters exhibit evidence for systematic variation across the host galaxy star-formation rate - stellar mass (SFR$-M_\star$) plane, motivating the incorporation of galaxy SFR information in cosmological inference. SFRs are commonly estimated via spectral energy distribution (SED) fitting with far-infrared (FIR) measurements to account for dust-obscured star formation. Such FIR coverage will, however, be limited for upcoming time-domain surveys such as the Rubin Observatory Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST), necessitating the use of alternative SFR tracers. Here, we reconstruct the SFR - $M_\star$ plane using 1.4 GHz diagnostics, to test the consistency of host classifications against FIR-constrained SED-based estimates. Within this plane, SN Ia host galaxies are divided into three regions: Region 1 (low-mass), Region 2 (high-mass star-forming) and Region 3 (high-mass passive). We find that ${\sim}84$ per cent of SN hosts retain identical region assignments when using radio versus FIR-constrained SED-derived SFRs. Measuring SN Ia nuisance parameters ($α,β, M$) within each subregion, we find consistent values between the two SFR - $M_\star$ plane reconstructions, indicating limited sensitivity to SFR estimator choice, with the largest deviations in Region 3 at ${\sim}1.1σ$. Across the three 1.4 GHz SFR - $M_\star$ subregions, we confirm the region-dependent variation in SN Ia standardisation parameters - particularly $β$ - reported in our earlier SED-based analysis. With near-complete radio coverage of the LSST footprint anticipated from current and forthcoming radio continuum surveys (e.g., Square Kilometre Array), radio SFR calibrations will become an increasingly useful and scalable approach to host galaxy classification, supporting the construction of robust SN Ia subsamples for precision cosmology.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that radio-based (1.4 GHz) star-formation rate estimates reliably reproduce the SFR-M* plane classification for SN Ia host galaxies compared to FIR-constrained SED fitting (~84% agreement), and yield consistent SN Ia standardisation parameters across subregions, supporting radio SFR tracers as a viable alternative for upcoming surveys like LSST that lack FIR coverage.
AI.motivation: SN Ia standardisation parameters vary across the host galaxy SFR-M* plane, motivating the need for SFR information in cosmological inference. However, FIR measurements needed for SED-based SFR estimates will be limited for upcoming surveys like LSST, requiring alternative SFR tracers.
AI.method: Reconstruct the SFR-M* plane using 1.4 GHz radio diagnostics and compare host galaxy region classifications (low-mass, high-mass star-forming, high-mass passive) and SN Ia nuisance parameters (α, β, M) against FIR-constrained SED-based estimates.
AI.result: ~84% of SN hosts retain identical region classifications; SN Ia nuisance parameters are consistent between the two SFR estimators, with the largest deviation in Region 3 at ~1.1σ. Region-dependent variation in standardisation parameters, particularly β, is confirmed.
AI.conclusion: Radio SFR calibrations are a scalable and increasingly viable alternative for host galaxy classification in the absence of FIR data, especially given the near-complete radio coverage of LSST from current and forthcoming surveys like SKA, enabling robust SN Ia subsamples for precision cosmology.

title: The faint voice of a radio-weak BL Lacertae: modeling the broadband emission of WISE~J141046.00+740511.2
comment: 7 pages, 4 figures, accepted for publication in A&A
summary: The WISE source, J141046.00+740511.2, has been recently observed from radio to $γ$ rays. Although the optical spectrum is consistent with a BL Lacertae (BL Lac) object, the source displays unusually weak radio emission, which challenges standard interpretations. Our aim is to understand the origin of the broadband emission from J141046.00+740511.2, using a leptonic model of an extended jet. To obtain the distribution of electrons along the conical jet, we solved a steady-state convective transport equation. Emissivities were computed along the jet and integrated over the cone volume to obtain the observed flux. Our model successfully reproduces the observed multiwavelength spectral energy distribution from radio to $γ$ rays and naturally accounts for the source's low radio flux without invoking extra emission zones. We also reproduce the mid-IR emission within the same framework. These results demonstrate that extended jet leptonic models can robustly describe the broadband physics of radio-weak BL Lacs.

AI.tldr: The paper models the broadband emission of the radio-weak BL Lac object J141046.00+740511.2 using a leptonic extended jet model, successfully reproducing its multiwavelength SED from radio to γ-rays without extra emission zones.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin of the broadband emission from the unusually radio-weak BL Lac object J141046.00+740511.2, which challenges standard BL Lac interpretations.
AI.method: A leptonic model of an extended conical jet was employed. The steady-state convective transport equation was solved to obtain the electron distribution along the jet. Emissivities were computed along the jet and integrated over the cone volume to derive the observed flux.
AI.result: The model successfully reproduces the observed SED from radio to γ-rays and naturally accounts for the low radio flux and mid-IR emission without invoking extra emission zones.
AI.conclusion: Extended jet leptonic models can robustly describe the broadband physics of radio-weak BL Lacs.

title: Chemical Taxonomy of $ω$~Centauri: Ten Populations Reveal a Multi-Phase Enrichment History
comment: Submitted to MNRAS. 36 pages, 30 figures, 4 tables
summary: $ω$~Centauri, the most massive globular cluster in the Milky Way, exhibits a level of stellar population complexity that has long resisted a unified chemical characterisation. We exploit high-resolution near-infrared spectroscopy from the Milky Way Mapper survey (MWM DR19) to construct one of the largest homogeneously analysed samples of $ω$~Cen members to date. Applying Ward-linkage hierarchical clustering in a seven-dimensional chemical abundance space, without prior assumptions on population number or boundaries, we identify ten chemically distinct stellar populations. Their nucleosynthetic signatures trace four enrichment channels: iron-peak, $α$-element, CNO-cycle, and high-temperature proton-capture processes. The populations organise into two dominant groups separated by a large light-element spread at a modest iron baseline, consistent with AGB-driven self-enrichment. This dichotomy reflects distinct enrichment pathways: core-collapse supernovae establish the iron baseline, while AGB stars dominate light-element and $s$-process enrichment. A decoupled rise in $s$-process abundances relative to iron-peak elements, together with sub-dominant Type~Ia contributions across all metallicities, indicates evolution on timescales shorter than the characteristic Type~Ia delay time. One intermediate-metallicity population retains a primordial composition, providing evidence for spatially segregated enrichment within the progenitor. The most metal-rich component may trace star formation continuing after accretion into the Milky Way halo. All populations lie in the accreted regime of the $[\mathrm{Al/Fe}]$--$[\mathrm{Mg/Mn}]$ plane, supporting an ex-situ origin. These results reinforce the interpretation of $ω$~Cen as the remnant nucleus of an accreted dwarf galaxy and provide a framework for future chemo-dynamical studies.

AI.tldr: This paper uses high-resolution near-infrared spectroscopy from the Milky Way Mapper survey (MWM DR19) to construct a large homogeneously analysed sample of ω Centauri members. Applying unsupervised hierarchical clustering in seven-dimensional chemical abundance space, they identify ten chemically distinct populations tracing four enrichment channels (iron-peak, α-element, CNO-cycle, high-temperature proton-capture). The results support ω Cen as the remnant nucleus of an accreted dwarf galaxy, with evidence for AGB-driven self-enrichment, sub-dominant Type Ia SN contribution, and a chemically primordial intermediate-metallicity population.
AI.motivation: ω Centauri exhibits extreme stellar population complexity that has resisted a unified chemical characterisation. The authors aim to provide a large-scale, homogeneous chemical abundance analysis to disentangle its enrichment history and clarify its origin.
AI.method: High-resolution near-infrared spectroscopy from Milky Way Mapper (MWM DR19); Ward-linkage hierarchical clustering in a seven-dimensional chemical abundance space (no prior assumptions on population number or boundaries) to identify chemically distinct stellar populations.
AI.result: Ten chemically distinct populations identified; four enrichment channels (iron-peak, α-element, CNO-cycle, high-temperature proton-capture); two dominant groups with large light-element spread at modest iron baseline; decoupled s-process rise relative to iron-peak; sub-dominant Type Ia SN across all metallicities; one intermediate-metallicity population retains primordial composition; most metal-rich component may trace post-accretion star formation.
AI.conclusion: ω Cen is the remnant nucleus of an accreted dwarf galaxy. Core-collapse SNe set the iron baseline while AGB stars dominate light-element and s-process enrichment. The chemical dichotomy, decoupled s-process, and primordial population support a self-enriched, spatially segregated progenitor with an ex-situ origin.
